ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a fried chicken food product class; the mashed potato component is not separately anchored. Composite set-meal ontology is not available; 'closest_parent' match type used for the dominant protein component.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers fried chicken meat. The mashed potato side dish is not captured in a single composite FoodEx2 code; the dominant protein component is used as the primary anchor, hence 'closest_parent' match type.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this composite set-meal food item.

serving.description: 1 set meal (2 pieces fried chicken + mashed potato)
serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving consists of 2 pieces of fried chicken (approximately 220–240 g) accompanied by a standard portion of mashed potato (approximately 110–130 g), totalling approximately 350 g.

nutrient_source.title: Fried chicken (2 pc) with mashed potato — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) for the composite entry 'Fried chicken (2 pc) with mashed potato' (cr_id F08020025). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried chicken (NDB 05062) and mashed potato (NDB 11371) in proportion to the composite, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — mashed potato and fried chicken composite estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite set meal. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate source: mashed potato has a published GI of approximately 70–87 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021, Am J Clin Nutr). The fried chicken component contributes negligible carbohydrate and acts as a fat-protein moderator, lowering the effective composite GI. A blended GI of 65 is used as a conservative medium estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × available carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 65 × 29.3 g / 100 = 19.1 (serving size 350 g, carbohydrate 8.36 g/100g × 350 g = 29.3 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 461 mg/100 g; estimated 1,614 mg per 350 g serving — exceeds 40% of the 4,000 mg daily limit in a single meal
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this meal; if consumed, avoid additional salt, sauces, or condiments, and request grilled chicken with plain boiled potato to substantially reduce sodium and saturated fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Mashed potato is a high-GI starch (GI ~70–87); composite meal GI estimated at 65 with glycemic load of 19.1 per serving — classified as high glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this meal due to the high glycemic load from mashed potato and the fat from deep-frying, which impairs insulin sensitivity; substituting with grilled chicken and a low-GI side such as steamed vegetables or brown rice is strongly recommended.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat ~2.3 g/100 g, trans fat ~0.2 g/100 g, cholesterol ~55 mg/100 g, and sodium 461 mg/100 g collectively elevate LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid deep-fried chicken meals; the combination of saturated fat, trans fat from frying oil, and high sodium significantly increases LDL cholesterol and arterial blood pressure, and grilled or baked chicken with steamed vegetables is a heart-healthier alternative.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and trans fats that are pro-inflammatory; butter in mashed potato adds saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried foods as AGEs and trans fats exacerbate joint inflammation; request grilled chicken and mashed potato prepared with olive oil instead of butter.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Chicken (2 Pieces) with Mashed Potato is a popular set meal widely available at fast-food outlets, hawker centres, and food courts across Singapore. The dish consists of two pieces of bone-in chicken — typically a drumstick and a thigh or breast — coated in a seasoned wheat-flour batter and deep-fried in oil until golden and crispy. The chicken exterior is crunchy and well-seasoned, while the interior remains juicy and tender. It is served alongside a portion of creamy mashed potato, typically prepared with butter and milk, giving it a smooth, rich texture. Per 100 g, the composite meal provides 156 kcal, 12.48 g protein, 8.05 g fat, and 8.36 g carbohydrate, with sodium at a notably high 461 mg. The mashed potato component is a high-glycaemic-index starch (GI ~70–87), making the overall meal glycaemically significant with an estimated composite GI of 65 and a glycemic load of approximately 19 per 350 g serving. Clinically, this meal is of concern for individuals managing hypertension (high sodium), type 2 diabetes (high glycemic load, fat-impaired insulin sensitivity), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol). The deep-frying process also generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which are pro-inflammatory and relevant for rheumatoid arthritis patients. Despite its high protein content, the overall nutritional profile is energy-dense and nutrient-poor relative to its caloric contribution. It is commonly consumed as a working lunch or casual dinner in Singapore and is generally considered halal-certified at major chains.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) canonical entry F08020025 with a stated confidence of 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, and cholesterol are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy proportional composition of fried chicken and mashed potato; these secondary fields are marked accordingly. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.3 g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (3.1 g/100g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.8 g/100g), trans_fat_g (0.2 g/100g), potassium_mg (290 mg/100g), and cholesterol_mg (55 mg/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy proportional composition of fried chicken (NDB 05062) and mashed potato (NDB 11371) blended at approximately 65:35 ratio by weight; HPB does not separately report these fields for this composite entry. 2. calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific composite set-meal entry are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a matched composite as of 2026-04-16. 3. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this exact composite meal was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. Halal status reflects common commercial preparation at major Singapore fast-food chains; individual outlet certification should be verified. 5. Trans fat value of 0.2 g/100g is an estimate from USDA SR Legacy fried chicken data; actual value may vary by frying oil used.
